item.title: Explosion at US embassy in Oslo may have been terrorism, Norway police say
item.description: Police say they are investigating other motives after the blast caused minor damage to the embassy building.

item.title: Tehran residents warned of acid rain after oil storage attack
item.description: Iran’s Red Crescent Society tells locals to avoid exposure as capital is engulfed in smoke

item.title: PM is standing up for UK interests, says Cooper after Trump criticism
item.description: The foreign secretary says the UK will not agree with the US "on every issue" after the US president attacks the PM's stance.

item.title:
U.S. CONSIDERS SPECIAL FORCES RAID TO SEIZE IRAN’S URANIUM
The U.S. and Israel have discussed sending special operations forces into Iran to secure its stockpile of highly enriched uranium, according to officials familiar with the talks.
The target is roughly 450 kilograms of uranium enriched to 60%, which could be upgraded to weapons-grade within weeks. Preventing Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon is a key objective of President Donald Trump.
Any mission would likely involve U.S., Israeli, or joint special forces, possibly alongside nuclear experts and inspectors from the International Atomic Energy Agency. Options include removing the uranium from Iran or diluting it on site.
Officials say the operation would only happen after Iran’s military is sufficiently weakened, due to the risks of entering heavily fortified underground facilities.
U.S. officials believe most of the uranium is buried at nuclear sites in Isfahan, with smaller amounts at Fordow and Natanz. If fully enriched to weapons grade, the stockpile could theoretically produce up to 11 nuclear bombs.
The plan under discussion is not a large invasion, officials say, but small targeted special-operations raids designed to secure or neutralize the material.
item.description:
U.S. CONSIDERS SPECIAL FORCES RAID TO SEIZE IRAN’S URANIUM

The U.S. and Israel have discussed sending special operations forces into Iran to secure its stockpile of highly enriched uranium, according to officials familiar with the talks.

The target is roughly 450 kilograms of uranium enriched to 60%, which could be upgraded to weapons-grade within weeks. Preventing Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon is a key objective of President Donald Trump.

Any mission would likely involve U.S., Israeli, or joint special forces, possibly alongside nuclear experts and inspectors from the International Atomic Energy Agency. Options include removing the uranium from Iran or diluting it on site.

Officials say the operation would only happen after Iran’s military is sufficiently weakened, due to the risks of entering heavily fortified underground facilities.

U.S. officials believe most of the uranium is buried at nuclear sites in Isfahan, with smaller amounts at Fordow and Natanz. If fully enriched to weapons grade, the stockpile could theoretically produce up to 11 nuclear bombs.

The plan under discussion is not a large invasion, officials say, but small targeted special-operations raids designed to secure or neutralize the material.

item.title: U.S. WEIGHS SENDING SPECIAL FORCES TO SEIZE IRAN'S NUCLEAR STOCKPILE: AXIOS
item.description: U.S. WEIGHS SENDING SPECIAL FORCES TO SEIZE IRAN'S NUCLEAR STOCKPILE: AXIOS

item.title: The guards rise again: the force still shaping Iran
item.description: Should the regime survive, hardliners believe the IRGC’s influence will be ever greater

item.title: ‘It’s our moment’: rival exiles lobby the US to lead Iran after the war
item.description: The son of the ousted shah and a longstanding rebel group spend big in Washington in hopes of ruling in Tehran

item.title: Gulf businesses buy up political violence insurance as conflict spreads
item.description: Data centres, energy projects and hotels move to limit potential losses

item.title: America chose this war — and must now choose how to end it
item.description: However this conflict concludes, the US and Iran’s new leaders will have to revisit the same issues that sparked hostilities

item.title: ‘Most dangerous job on earth’: the life of an Iranian ballistic missile crew
item.description: Tehran’s missiles are key to its retaliation against Israel and the US. But launching them brings an immediate risk of death

item.title: Trump says he does not need UK aircraft carriers to win war with Iran
item.description: The remarks come after a public back-and-forth between Trump and Sir Keir Starmer over UK involvement the US's joint offensive with Israel against Iran.

item.title: Jessie Buckley says acting helped her overcome eating disorder as a teenager
item.description: The Oscar-frontrunner says acting is "like water to me" as she credits her craft with helping her mental health.

item.title: 'Our children paid the ultimate price' – How the Dunblane school shooting changed Britain
item.description: A new BBC documentary charts how the murders of 16 children and their teacher in March 1996 led to a handgun ban in the UK.

item.title: Trump hits out at Starmer over UK reluctance to support Iran attacks
item.description: Comments come as London considers deploying aircraft carrier to the region

item.title: The legacy of Holly and Jessica's murders: Soham 'won't waste its breath' on Huntley

item.title: US faced with few good options to tamp down surging oil prices
item.description: Experts say reopening Strait of Hormuz remains most viable option as ideas such as influencing futures market fade

item.title: A week of war in charts: the impact on the US
item.description: The conflict with Iran is unpopular with the American public and is roiling markets and the economy

item.title: The bombing of Tehran — in maps and satellite images
item.description: Israeli and US strikes have targeted regime infrastructure and also struck civilian sites. Here are some of the key buildings hit

item.title: The war of unintended consequences
item.description: Israel and the US have achieved many military aims in Iran, but the unpredictable fallout poses threats to everyone

item.title: Trump’s war on Iran is spreading. Where does it stop?
item.description: US allies in the Arab world have been plunged into a conflict they neither wanted nor consented to. Historian Eugene Rogan on what it means for the Middle East
